title: The Untethered Sole

jokes.text: Sorry, Gob. Looks like there isn't gonna be enough for you.
jokes.setup: Family is applying suntan lotion at the beach house in a flashback sequence
jokes.punchline: Lucille casually excludes Gob from the sunscreen, framing her neglect as a logistical problem rather than favoritism
jokes.explanation: Classic Lucille parenting joke. The casualness of 'there isn't gonna be enough' is perfect — she doesn't say she doesn't want to give him any, just frames it as scarcity. Strong character integration: this could only be Lucille to Gob.

jokes.text: I never should have done Lindsay first. Lindsay's having a widening spurt.
jokes.setup: Lucille is applying sunscreen to the children
jokes.punchline: She comments on Lindsay 'having a widening spurt' — a cruel reframing of a growth spurt as weight gain, delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: 'Widening spurt' is a beautifully economical coinage — it sounds like a real parenting term, which makes it land harder. The joke is a perfect encapsulation of Lucille's cruelty toward Lindsay and her ability to weaponize clinical-sounding language. High quotability.

jokes.text: Please, I don't need any suntan lotion. Dad never wears any. / Yeah. And he's doing fine in the lady department.
jokes.setup: Buster declines sunscreen by citing his father as a role model
jokes.punchline: Another sibling agrees, but interprets 'doing fine' as referring to George Sr.'s romantic life — a non sequitur that implies the kids know something embarrassing about their father
jokes.explanation: Decent misdirection — the expected meaning of 'doing fine' (health) is swapped for 'lady department' (affairs), implying George Sr.'s infidelity is known to the kids. Works as a quick joke but not especially distinctive.

jokes.text: Well, Buster doesn't know what the hell he's talking about! / I think Buster was actually talking about Mom.
jokes.setup: George Sr. vehemently denies what Buster said about 'the lady department,' assuming Buster meant his mistresses
jokes.punchline: Michael clarifies Buster was talking about Lucille (their mother), revealing George Sr.'s guilt and making his angry denial self-incriminating
jokes.explanation: Tight two-beat structure. George Sr.'s over-the-top defensive reaction is the setup; Michael's flat clarification is the punchline. Classic misdirection/reveal that exposes character through comic misunderstanding. Well-constructed.

jokes.text: Oh, yes. No, uh... I thought... you were talking about recent... Yes, thank you. Yes, she is, uh... She is quite the beauty.
jokes.setup: George Sr. tries to recover from exposing his guilt and pivot to complimenting Lucille
jokes.punchline: His verbal stumbling ('recent') implies he was thinking of recent affairs, and his eventual compliment of Lucille as 'quite the beauty' is transparently forced
jokes.explanation: Continues the previous joke's momentum. The word 'recent' slipping out is a nice detail that rewards attentive viewers. Decent cringe comedy from George Sr.'s inability to recover gracefully.

jokes.text: Well, that's the downside of having a mother who never ages.
jokes.setup: The kids lament that they don't stand a chance in the family beauty pageant
jokes.punchline: One child frames Lucille's agelessness as a 'downside' — absurd because it's both a compliment and an indictment of a pageant that pits parents against children
jokes.explanation: The Bluth family having a beauty pageant judged by the parents is already absurd; the child's sincere complaint that Lucille never ages compounds the absurdity. Good economy.

jokes.text: It must be something in these cigarettes.
jokes.setup: A sibling says Lucille never ages
jokes.punchline: Lucille attributes her youthfulness to her cigarettes — an absurd inversion of reality delivered with total sincerity
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best one-liners. The joke inverts common health wisdom — cigarettes are famously aging and deadly — and Lucille credits them as her beauty secret with complete deadpan sincerity. Maximum economy (six words), strong character integration (only Lucille would say this and mean it), high quotability. Justifies 8+ score.

jokes.text: It doesn't hurt that you guys are the judges. / They are? / You guys are the judges?
jokes.setup: A child reveals the parents are the judges of the pageant they also compete in
jokes.punchline: George Sr. and Lucille are genuinely surprised — they apparently didn't realize they were judges, suggesting the pageant is so dysfunctional nobody has a clear picture of the rules
jokes.explanation: The joke lands on the absurdity that the parents didn't know they were judges of their own family competition. Good beat but fairly quick payoff.

jokes.text: Kids, your mother and I have discussed it, and you're all old enough now to actually impress us, so... we're out of the running.
jokes.setup: George Sr. builds up to what sounds like a meaningful parental announcement
jokes.punchline: The 'big news' is that they're withdrawing from competing against their own children — which reframes the entire premise as insane: parents shouldn't need to quit a pageant against their kids
jokes.explanation: The pause before 'we're out of the running' is the key — the pacing lets the absurdity of the Bluths competing against their children in a pageant fully land. The matter-of-fact delivery amplifies the dysfunction.

jokes.text: And it can't just be push-ups. / Yeah, it won't just be push-ups.
jokes.setup: George Sr. warns the kids their talent can't just be push-ups
jokes.punchline: Buster — whose talent clearly would have been push-ups — immediately agrees that it won't JUST be push-ups, implying push-ups are still in the plan
jokes.explanation: Perfect Buster joke. The structure is elegant: George Sr. sets a bar, Buster technically clears it while confirming he's still doing push-ups. 'Won't just be' is doing all the work — implying push-ups are foundational to whatever he's planning. Strong character integration and economy.

jokes.text: I'm sure you'll all be thin by Thursday, Lindsay.
jokes.setup: Lucille wishes the kids good luck in the pageant
jokes.punchline: She singles out Lindsay with a 'wish' that she'll be thin by Thursday — simultaneously wishing her luck and insulting her weight
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in Lucille's passive-aggressive cruelty. The line is framed as encouragement but lands as an insult. 'By Thursday' is a perfect specific detail — it implies Lindsay has so far to go that a deadline must be set. High character integration; only Lucille delivers this with sweetness.

jokes.text: Oh, come on. He still has to wear that dog collar? / It's not a dog collar. It's Buster's Gentleman Collar.
jokes.setup: It's revealed Buster wears a collar (like a dog) attached to a leash
jokes.punchline: Lucille insists on calling it a 'Gentleman Collar,' which both denies its obvious nature and elevates it with absurd dignity
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's strongest jokes. 'Gentleman Collar' is an inspired piece of naming — the juxtaposition of 'Gentleman' with what is explicitly a dog collar captures the entire Lucille/Buster dynamic in two words. It's euphemistic, delusional, and perfectly in character. Highly quotable, strong rewatch value as it sets up the collar running gag. Justifies 8+ score.

jokes.text: And he barely even knows it's there. / Yeah, I barely know it's...
jokes.setup: Lucille claims Buster barely notices the collar
jokes.punchline: Buster begins to agree but trails off — implying he absolutely notices it but has been conditioned to deny it
jokes.explanation: The trailing-off is the joke — Buster's mid-sentence self-correction implies he very much knows it's there. The beat lands on Buster's deeply conditioned compliance with Lucille's reality distortion. Good character work.

jokes.text: Well, he's grown a little. [visual: the collar is visibly too tight on an adult Buster]
jokes.setup: We're in a flashback; the collar is from childhood. Now adult Buster is wearing the same collar.
jokes.punchline: Lucille acknowledges the collar is too small with masterful understatement — 'He's grown a little' — as if the only problem is a sizing issue, not that her adult son is in a collar
jokes.explanation: The visual of an adult Buster in an undersized childhood collar is inherently funny. Lucille's 'grown a little' acknowledges only the physical reality (it's tight) while ignoring the actual horror (her adult son is in a collar). Classic Lucille understatement.

jokes.text: You bought me a dorky, lame, stupid, gay magic kit? / What? No. This is for me.
jokes.setup: Michael sees a magic kit and assumes Gob bought it as a gift
jokes.punchline: Gob clarifies it's for himself — he bought a magic kit as an adult man for his own use — which is both more pathetic and more in-character
jokes.explanation: The reversal is clean — Michael's assumption makes the joke, but Gob's clarification deepens it. Gob buying himself a 'dorky, lame, stupid, gay magic kit' for a family talent show as an adult is perfectly in character. The rewatch bonus comes from knowing Gob's magic career trajectory.

jokes.text: Does it come with that bird? / No, I gotta get a bird. But I'll get a bird.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if the magic kit includes a bird prop
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'But I'll get a bird' is delivered with complete confidence and determination — foreshadowing/establishing his later habit of getting birds for tricks
jokes.explanation: Sets up the Franklin/dove recurring gag of Gob and birds. 'But I'll get a bird' has a wonderful confident resolve that implies Gob will absolutely follow through on this minor point. Strong rewatch value for fans who know where his bird obsession goes.

jokes.text: Mom tightened it when he tried to put that starfish in his mouth.
jokes.setup: Someone asks why Buster can't get past the window anymore (the collar tether)
jokes.punchline: Michael explains the collar was tightened as punishment for Buster trying to eat a starfish — an insane specific detail that explains nothing rationally but fits perfectly with Buster's childlike oral fixations
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'starfish' is what elevates this. Any object would be funny, but a starfish is perfect — it's a beach-appropriate, Buster-appropriate, deeply strange choice. The callback to Buster's sea-creature obsession (his hand, etc.) makes this reward long-term viewers. Justifies 8+ for its layered specificity and character integration.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, but he's had a good summer. He's made friends.'
jokes.setup: Concern that the window was tightened because of Buster's erratic behavior
jokes.punchline: Michael defends Buster by noting he's 'made friends' — as though reporting on a child at summer camp, about a grown adult
jokes.explanation: The infantilizing language applied to an adult is consistently funny in Buster's storylines. 'He's made friends' as a defense is both sweet and deeply sad.

jokes.text: Actually, Mother made the friend for me. So I named it after her: Lucille Ball.
jokes.setup: Michael says Buster has 'made friends' at the beach
jokes.punchline: Buster reveals his mother literally made/created the friend (presumably a stuffed animal or beach prop), and he named it 'Lucille Ball' — a pun combining Lucille Bluth with the comedian Lucille Ball, also calling her a ball
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best joke and one of the show's finest. It operates on multiple levels simultaneously: (1) Lucille made her son's only friend — pathetic and dark; (2) Buster named it after her, showing his total enmeshment; (3) 'Lucille Ball' is a perfect pun on Lucille Bluth and the actress; (4) it recontextualizes the beach ball as a Wilson-esque (Cast Away) friendship object; (5) it foreshadows Buster's relationship with his hook. Maximum character integration — this joke is ONLY possible with Buster and Lucille. Exceptional originality and structure. Clearly the episode's standout.

jokes.text: Is anybody else worried she's ruining us?
jokes.setup: Buster reveals Lucille manufactured his only friend and he named it after her
jokes.punchline: Michael voices what the audience is thinking — a moment of rare self-awareness from the Bluths about their mother's impact
jokes.explanation: Michael as the audience surrogate is a recurring role, and this is a clean execution of that function. The question is asked with genuine worry rather than sarcasm, which makes it funnier. Good economy.

jokes.text: Take that back! You take that back! We're normal! We're normal! / Okay. Down, down. / Easy, Buster. Easy, boy. / Sit.
jokes.setup: Michael asks if anyone is worried Lucille is ruining them
jokes.punchline: Buster has an explosive, dog-like reaction; the family calms him using commands ('Easy, boy,' 'Sit') as if he were an actual dog — completing the Gentleman Collar/dog metaphor
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the Gentleman Collar setup: the family treats Buster like a dog mid-outburst, and the commands ('Easy, boy,' 'Sit') complete the metaphor. The escalation from Michael's gentle question to Buster's explosive 'WE'RE NORMAL' is perfectly paced. 'Sit' as the final one-word punchline is outstanding economy. Justifies 8+ as the structural callback reward.

jokes.text: I would say wait for me, but it might be a couple of years.
jokes.setup: Michael says he'll go change into his suit and asks them to wait
jokes.punchline: Tobias responds with a cryptic comment suggesting he can't join them for 'a couple of years' — a self-aware reference to his legal troubles/restrictions that he delivers with inappropriate cheerfulness
jokes.explanation: Decent but underpowered. The joke implies Tobias has legal restrictions but the setup is thin in a flashback context and the punchline is vague enough to not fully land.

jokes.text: I think Buster wants to go take a swim. [beat] I do! I've never even got my suit wet from the outside.
jokes.setup: Gob suggests Buster wants to swim
jokes.punchline: Buster confirms enthusiastically — but his qualifier 'from the outside' implies his suit has gotten wet from the inside (i.e., from bodily fluids/accidents), delivered with total innocence
jokes.explanation: Exceptional Buster line. The phrase 'from the outside' is doing enormous work — it implies a history of inside-wetness that Buster states matter-of-factly, without embarrassment or awareness of the implication. The contrast between his enthusiasm and the horror of what he's revealing is perfectly calibrated. High quotability, strong originality in phrasing. Justifies 8+ score.

jokes.text: Magic is for girls. / And your bird's gonna die, Michael. / And when it does, it'll go back to... whence it came. / Back to whence it came.
jokes.setup: The children are discussing their talents for the pageant
jokes.punchline: Lucille dismisses magic as 'for girls'; Michael prophecies the bird's death; George Sr. adds the ominous 'whence it came' with gravitas, then immediately has to repeat it when no one reacts, deflating his dramatic moment
jokes.explanation: Three-part joke with a solid structure. The repetition of 'Back to whence it came' (George Sr. having to repeat his own dramatic line) is a good undercut. Has rewatch value as foreshadowing for Gob's ongoing bird-death gag in the series.

jokes.text: Mr. Bluth was arrested last night in an elevator in Newport.
jokes.setup: News report describing Buster's arrest
jokes.punchline: The specific detail 'in an elevator in Newport' is delivered with total matter-of-factness — the specificity implies this is not the first such arrest
jokes.explanation: Functional exposition joke; the elevator specificity is a mild chuckle but nothing special.

jokes.text: This is what happens when you're rich and white in America. You happen to be caught on video, dumping some large person-type thing into the bay, and nobody thinks, 'I wonder how long that guy has been working for the real killer?'
jokes.setup: George Sr. complains about the family's treatment by the media and police
jokes.punchline: He frames the injustice as being 'rich and white' — meaning they should be MORE protected, not less — inverting the usual meaning of that phrase as a complaint about privilege
jokes.explanation: Sharp satirical inversion. George Sr. using 'rich and white in America' as a victimhood complaint — because he expects MORE impunity — is both a character joke and a pointed social observation. The follow-up ('working for the real killer') layers absurdist conspiracy logic on top. Strong structure.

jokes.text: Yeah, and they jumped right to 'He's the killer,' and then don't think to add: 'but it's nice he's giving the help the night off.'
jokes.setup: The family complains police jumped to conclusions about Buster
jokes.punchline: Lucille's defense is that Buster was being considerate to 'the help' (whoever he allegedly killed/disposed of) by 'giving them the night off' — reframing murder as employee relations
jokes.explanation: A superb Lucille joke. Her defense of Buster is that he was giving 'the help' the night off — she can't help but refer to a potential murder victim as 'the help,' which perfectly encapsulates her class obliviousness. The reframing of potential murder as considerate employment management is both darkly funny and deeply character-specific. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: No, they're jealous. They are jealous. And they won't stop until one of us is a murderer. Today, it's Buster. Tomorrow... No, I guess it'll stop today. Today's the last day.
jokes.setup: Tobias tries to rally the family against the media
jokes.punchline: He begins a dramatic 'today/tomorrow' escalation, then immediately corrects himself — realizing if Buster is the murderer, the witch hunt ends — and concludes 'today's the last day' with cheerful finality
jokes.explanation: The mid-speech self-correction is the joke's engine. Tobias starts a rousing 'first they came for...' style speech, then his own logic deflates it mid-sentence. 'Today's the last day' delivered as a conclusion rather than a failure is perfectly absurdist. Good structure.

jokes.text: Really, we're just asking this Asian gentleman and his wife...? To move over to that group of chairs there, freeing up the end seat, which I would love to take a shot at sitting in.
jokes.setup: George Sr. calls for family unity while in the police station
jokes.punchline: He immediately pivots to asking a random Asian couple to move so he can have their end seat — demonstrating his actual priorities mid-speech about togetherness
jokes.explanation: Good character joke — George Sr.'s self-interest immediately undercutting his call for unity. 'I would love to take a shot at sitting in' as a goal for the end seat is characteristically entitled. The Chinese/Asian detail becomes a running thread in the episode.

jokes.text: My friend spoke to the DA's office, and my lawyers were able to reduce my sentence to time served. Bad news? I can't spend any time with anybody under 25.
jokes.setup: The family asks what was found out about Buster's case
jokes.punchline: The speaker reveals THEIR OWN legal news (sentence reduced, can't be around under-25s) before being redirected to the actual question about Buster — complete self-absorption
jokes.explanation: The misdirection of answering a question about someone else with personal legal news is a clean sitcom move. 'Can't spend any time with anybody under 25' is a dark, suggestive detail delivered as an inconvenience rather than a scandal.

jokes.text: Son of a bitch! [evidence supervisor damages stair car in parking spot]
jokes.setup: The narrator explains the stair car (key evidence) was abandoned in a parking spot assigned to evidence supervisor Eddie Cantrow
jokes.punchline: Before realizing it's evidence, Cantrow reacts with rage and damages the stair car — inadvertently destroying the evidence against Buster
jokes.explanation: A brilliant piece of Arrested Development plotting-as-comedy: the stair car is destroyed not by nefarious means but by a parking-spot-protective evidence supervisor who cares more about his spot than his job. The narrator's note that 'it had taken him a super long time to get a decent parking spot' humanizes and deepens the absurdity. Strong structure.

jokes.text: In fairness to him, it had taken him a super long time to get a decent parking spot that he didn't have to walk, like, a mile from.
jokes.setup: The evidence supervisor destroys key evidence because the stair car is in his spot
jokes.punchline: The narrator offers sympathetic context — the parking spot struggle — which makes the evidence destruction seem understandable, deflating legal gravity with mundane relatability
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan sympathy for someone who accidentally destroyed murder evidence over a parking spot is peak Arrested Development narrator work. The 'like, a mile from' adds casual authenticity that elevates the absurdity.

jokes.text: No, we... we didn't have him say anything. I said, 'Don't say a word. Put on your poker face.' Although Buster's poker face didn't really make him look like less of a murderer.
jokes.setup: The family learns Buster said nothing to police; they credit good legal advice
jokes.punchline: The lawyer admits Buster's 'poker face' makes him look MORE like a murderer — implying whatever Buster does to look innocent is somehow worse
jokes.explanation: Good Buster joke — we don't see the poker face but can perfectly imagine it. The setup of 'put on your poker face' being terrible legal advice for Buster specifically is funny. Rewatch bonus for imagining Tony Hale's face.

jokes.text: Mom, you had him on a leash until he was six. / Well, if that leash screwed him up so badly, why does he still want to wear it?
jokes.setup: Lucille claims she raised Buster 'by the book'; Michael challenges this
jokes.punchline: Lucille's defense is that if the leash was bad, why does Buster still want it? — completely ignoring that wanting to wear it is evidence of the damage, not proof against it
jokes.explanation: Lucille's argument is perfectly circular and self-incriminating — 'why does he still want to wear it?' is the most damning possible evidence of the harm she caused, presented as exculpatory. Her total inability to see this is peak Lucille. The logic is airtight in its wrongness. High quotability and rewatch value. Justifies 8+ score.

jokes.text: Even though they do say, in most cases, something happens in childhood and, usually, the person that raises them is to blame. But that's experts.
jokes.setup: Michael says it's not time to play the blame game regarding Buster's issues
jokes.punchline: Tobias agrees while immediately citing expert consensus that would implicate Lucille — then dismisses it as 'but that's experts,' as if expert opinion is irrelevant
jokes.explanation: 'But that's experts' as a dismissal of scientific consensus about child development is a sharp satirical jab. The structure — state damning truth, immediately dismiss it — is clean. Good Tobias obliviousness.

jokes.text: Don't you try to pin this on Lupe. She had her own family to raise, too.
jokes.setup: Tobias implies the person who raised Buster is to blame for his issues
jokes.punchline: Lucille defends Lupe (the housekeeper) — accidentally confirming she delegated Buster's upbringing to the housekeeper while simultaneously deflecting blame
jokes.explanation: A superb Lucille joke. By defending Lupe, she inadvertently reveals (1) Lupe raised Buster, not her, (2) she knows exactly who Tobias is implicating (but chooses the wrong person), and (3) she finds 'she had her own family' as a meaningful defense. Every word reveals character. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: I'm in the family, and it's gonna be crazy. It's gonna be a media circus, Tobias. / Then I should be its ringmaster.
jokes.setup: Someone suggests Tobias shouldn't speak for the family to the media
jokes.punchline: Tobias responds to 'media circus' with 'then I should be its ringmaster' — extending the metaphor with total self-seriousness while ironically being the least qualified person
jokes.explanation: Tobias extending the 'media circus' metaphor to cast himself as ringmaster is solid but predictable. The joke is in the gap between his self-perception and his actual competence. Decent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: In fact, Tobias would've taken any job at that moment, as his current living arrangement was tense, indeed.
jokes.setup: Tobias aggressively volunteers to be family spokesman
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals via dry understatement that Tobias's home situation is 'tense' — implying he wants any excuse to be somewhere else
jokes.explanation: Functional narrator joke setting up the next scene. 'Tense, indeed' is a nice period choice but the joke is mostly setup for what follows.

jokes.text: Something that is really only clear without the quick editing and carefree score.
jokes.setup: The narrator introduces a scene of domestic chaos in Tobias's home
jokes.punchline: The narrator notes the chaos is 'only clear without the quick editing and carefree score' — a meta comment on how the show usually packages dysfunction as comedy, suggesting what follows is unusually raw
jokes.explanation: A genuinely clever meta-moment. The narrator critiques the show's own comedic packaging — 'quick editing and carefree score' — as tools that make dysfunction palatable. It momentarily breaks the fourth wall to make the audience aware of how comedy is constructed. Strong for Arrested Development's self-aware style.

jokes.text: How 'bout I put your head through this glass table? How funny would that be, young man?
jokes.setup: Someone tries to be funny at the dinner table; an adult responds with rage
jokes.punchline: The threat of physical violence is framed as a question about what's funny — deeply uncomfortable because it's delivered with apparent seriousness
jokes.explanation: The violence threat packaged as a comedy question ('how funny would that be?') is a solid dark joke. Effective cringe but not especially memorable in this episode.

jokes.text: There were six. There were six in here. Who the [bleep] took 'em?
jokes.setup: The domestic scene is already chaotic with fighting
jokes.punchline: The count of missing pills (presumably pain medication) — 'there were six' — adds a drug-abuse dimension to the family chaos, escalating the dysfunction
jokes.explanation: Escalation of the domestic chaos scene. The specific count ('there were six') is a good detail. Functions as one of several jokes in the chaotic sequence establishing how bad Tobias's home life is.

jokes.text: As you see, it's latex, so it can go right up your ass.
jokes.setup: Tobias appears after the chaotic home scene, apparently pitching himself as a spokesman
jokes.punchline: Tobias — in his characteristic double-entendre fashion — uses an entirely inappropriate phrase ('right up your ass') seemingly while explaining something non-sexual (possibly a costume or prop)
jokes.explanation: Classic Tobias double-entendre, delivered without awareness. The latex/ass combination is an escalation of his accidental innuendo running gag. 'As you see' suggesting he's demonstrating something makes the context even more inappropriate.

jokes.text: A young, one-armed, Iraq War vet suffering from PTSD, raised by a young mother who had him at 17, whose only flaw was that... she loved him... too much. / Actually, I was 37. / Well, no one would believe that.
jokes.setup: Tobias pitches his PR spin for Buster's defense to the family
jokes.punchline: Lucille corrects his age (37, not 17), and Michael immediately agrees the truth is unbelievable — both confirming Lucille's actual age and suggesting she looks old enough to believably have had a teen pregnancy
jokes.explanation: Three-beat joke with perfect structure. Tobias's spin (17, 'loved him too much') is revealed as false; Lucille corrects to 37 (not a sympathetic number but accurate); Michael's 'no one would believe that' is a savage aside that implies 37 sounds too old to be plausible, insulting Lucille while seeming to agree with Tobias. The ellipses in 'loved him... too much' are doing great work. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Every time you handle things, they're mishandled. / Yeah, your mom's right. I mean, lately, you're just doing everything badly. / Yeah, Dad, nail his ass. / I'll pile on to nailing that ass, Dad.
jokes.setup: Lucille dismisses Michael's competence; others join in
jokes.punchline: Tobias volunteering to 'pile on to nailing that ass' is an inadvertent sexual double entendre; combined with the pile-on dynamic, the joke escalates through each additional voice
jokes.explanation: The family pile-on is a good structural joke, and Tobias's 'pile on to nailing that ass' adds an accidental innuendo layer. The escalation of each person joining in has good rhythm.

jokes.text: Oh! I should have led with this. I have been officially disbarred. I can't handle Buster's case or anybody under 25. Saves me an awkward conversation.
jokes.setup: Michael suggests getting Buster a good lawyer
jokes.punchline: Barry reveals he's disbarred — and rather than being ashamed, he notes it 'saves me an awkward conversation,' framing disbarment as a convenient excuse
jokes.explanation: 'Saves me an awkward conversation' is the punchline and it's a good one — framing disbarment as personally convenient. 'I should have led with this' is also strong, implying he sat through relevant discussion knowing he was disbarred. Good Barry characterization.

jokes.text: The Guilty Guys [narrator introduces five elite lawyers with absurdly over-produced dossiers]
jokes.setup: Michael asks for the best criminal lawyers available
jokes.punchline: The narrator introduces each lawyer with an overblown TV-drama-style biography, parodying legal procedural shows while delivering information about absurdly over-credentialed people for what is essentially a family farce
jokes.explanation: The Guilty Guys sequence is a sustained parody of legal procedural show introductions — each biography is more elaborate than the last. The joke is partly structural (this level of production for Bluth family legal problems) and partly in specific details like 'wasn't afraid to get a little DNA on her' and 'his beloved Raiders.'

jokes.text: She was an expert who wasn't afraid to get a little DNA on her.
jokes.setup: Narrator describes Shara Winfield's forensic DNA expertise
jokes.punchline: 'Wasn't afraid to get a little DNA on her' works as both a professional compliment and an unintentional sexual innuendo in the procedural parody context
jokes.explanation: The DNA line is tightly economical — it works on two levels without being explicit. In the context of parody procedural narration, the double meaning is well-placed. High economy score.

jokes.text: David G. 'G-Man' Barnes cared about but one thing above the law: his beloved Raiders.
jokes.setup: The narrator builds to what the street-smart lawyer cares about above the law
jokes.punchline: The anticipated answer (justice, the streets, something noble) is replaced with the Oakland Raiders — a specific, mundane sports loyalty as his defining characteristic
jokes.explanation: The misdirection setup (mean streets, single mother, justice, Jesus) promises a noble punchline. The Oakland Raiders as his one passion above law is a perfect comedic deflation. The specificity of the Raiders (not just 'football') adds authenticity to the absurdity.

jokes.text: And I think I might have gotten Jack and David mixed up.
jokes.setup: The narrator finishes introducing the five lawyers with elaborate individual backstories
jokes.punchline: The narrator casually admits he may have mixed up two of the lawyers he just spent two minutes carefully introducing — undermining all the investment in the procedural parody
jokes.explanation: This is among the best meta-narrator jokes in the series. After a full elaborate parody procedural introduction with specific biographical details for each lawyer, the narrator immediately undermines it all with 'I think I might have gotten Jack and David mixed up.' It retroactively destroys the credibility of everything that just happened, collapses the procedural parody from the inside, and is delivered with perfect casualness. Economy is near-perfect (one sentence). Justifies 8+ score handily.

jokes.text: 'Very expensive, but very, very good — especially if you're guilty.'
jokes.setup: The Guilty Guys have been introduced as having 'a perfect record defending real criminals who'd done real crimes'
jokes.punchline: George Sr. explains they're especially good 'if you're guilty' — and when Michael says Buster isn't guilty, George Sr. says 'Then I would go with the cheaper option'
jokes.explanation: A two-part joke that builds perfectly. First beat: 'especially if you're guilty' — which suggests these lawyers are so good they can get guilty people off. Second beat: Michael says Buster isn't guilty / George: 'Then I'd go with the cheaper option' — which implies that for innocent people, any lawyer will do. The logic is airtight and deeply cynical. Justifies 8+ — this is a precision-built satirical joke about criminal justice.

jokes.text: We want the best. / Well, how are we gonna pay for that? We don't have any money. It's all tied up in Fakeblock. Thanks, Michael.
jokes.setup: The family decides they want the best lawyers
jokes.punchline: George Sr. immediately blames Michael for the family's finances being tied up in Fakeblock, with a passive-aggressive 'Thanks, Michael'
jokes.explanation: Functional running gag of the family blaming Michael. The repeated 'Thanks, Michael' chorus that follows is funnier than the individual instance.

jokes.text: 'Thanks, Michael.' / 'Thanks, Michael.' [repeated by multiple family members unprompted]
jokes.setup: George Sr. blames Michael for finances
jokes.punchline: Multiple family members pile on with 'Thanks, Michael' as a chorus — transforming a single complaint into a ritualistic family blame-shift
jokes.explanation: The chorus of 'Thanks, Michael' is a great running gag escalation. The rhythm of multiple family members independently but simultaneously reaching the same passive-aggressive phrase captures the Bluth family dynamic perfectly. Economy: maximum impact from two words.

jokes.text: 'Get us back on our feet.' / They're... They're threatening our feet?
jokes.setup: George Sr. relays a message from Chinese investors using the phrase 'get us back on our feet'
jokes.punchline: Michael takes the idiom literally — 'threatening our feet' — establishing a running gag about Chinese investors literally threatening to cut off feet
jokes.explanation: Sets up a running gag that pays off multiple times through the episode. Michael's literal reading of 'get back on our feet' as a foot-severing threat is a perfect Arrested Development layered joke — it's simultaneously about Buster's hand (severed limb precedent in the show) and idiom literalism. Strong rewatch value.

jokes.text: I will say this for Saddam Hussein. With him, you didn't have to guess. It was very direct. 'I'll chop this off, I'll chop that off.' Right. With him, you knew where you stood. Or... Or couldn't.
jokes.setup: The family discusses the Chinese investors' threatening messages
jokes.punchline: George Sr. praises Saddam Hussein for being direct about which body parts he'd chop off; the final 'or couldn't' is a reference to those who had their legs/feet chopped — making the idiom 'knew where you stood' literally impossible for Hussein's victims
jokes.explanation: Exceptional dark wordplay. George Sr. casually praising Hussein for communications clarity is already funny; the punchline 'you knew where you stood. Or... couldn't' is a masterful callback to the foot/standing idiom — because Hussein's victims literally couldn't stand after having their feet cut off. Three layers: (1) George Sr. character comedy, (2) dark political humor, (3) the idiom made literal. The pause before 'Or couldn't' is perfect timing on the page. Justifies 8+ clearly.

jokes.text: There's no way the killer was Ann Veal. No match. / DNA don't lie. / Take her off the board.
jokes.setup: The Guilty Guys are doing their first week of work on Buster's case
jokes.punchline: Their major breakthrough: definitively ruling out Ann Veal as the killer — which was presumably not a serious theory — and billing $7,000 for it
jokes.explanation: Excellent use of the Ann Veal character (her recurrence makes the callback land). The Guilty Guys' first result — eliminating a clearly innocent character who was never a suspect — parodies procedural drama while commenting on how lawyers bill. 'DNA don't lie' and 'Take her off the board' perform procedural seriousness. The $7,000 price tag delivers the punchline.

jokes.text: The Guilty Guys' first week of 'leaving no stone unturned' consists entirely of ruling out Ann Veal — for $7,000
jokes.setup: Michael hired the expensive Guilty Guys to work on Buster's case
jokes.punchline: Their first week of work produced a $7,000 bill for conclusively establishing that Ann Veal had nothing to do with Buster's case — an almost comically irrelevant result
jokes.explanation: The Guilty Guys being expensive and producing absurd results is a running gag throughout the episode. The specific choice of Ann Veal — a minor recurring character — as the person they spent a week eliminating is a great callback for longtime viewers. 'DNA don't lie. Take her off the board.' delivered with complete seriousness about an obviously irrelevant lead is pitch-perfect.

jokes.text: I mean, Jesus Christ, $7,000 to find out that Ann Veal had nothing to do with Buster's case?
jokes.setup: The Guilty Guys bill $7,000 for ruling out Ann Veal
jokes.punchline: Michael's incredulous reaction confirms and amplifies the absurdity of the previous beat
jokes.explanation: Functional reaction beat that amplifies the Ann Veal billing joke. Michael as the straight man completes the structure. Competent but not as strong as the setup.

jokes.text: You sold the company? / A little. / You can't sell a company a little. That's like saying you're a little bit tired. / You don't think you can be a little bit tired?
jokes.setup: George Michael confronts Michael about selling Fakeblock
jokes.punchline: Michael's defense is 'a little,' then when called out, he deflects with a semantic debate about whether 'a little tired' is valid — completely dodging accountability
jokes.explanation: The exchange is tight and character-specific. Michael's 'a little' defense is wonderfully evasive; his pivot to 'you don't think you can be a little bit tired?' is a perfect dad-logic deflection. The semantic debate about fatigue as a way to avoid discussing selling a company is very Michael Bluth.

jokes.text: 'It's over now? A little.' — George Michael's 'a little' callback
jokes.setup: Michael's girlfriend says their relationship is over; Michael asks if it's 'over'
jokes.punchline: He echoes back 'A little' — using the same qualifier that was just established as logically impossible
jokes.explanation: The 'a little' callback works because it was just established as absurd, and now Michael uses it earnestly to describe the end of a relationship. The irony that he can't see his own contradiction is good character comedy.

jokes.text: Well, you know, I'd be careful of promising even a little. You know, some people, you give them an inch, and they take... / A foot. / They're threatening our feet? Is it a foot now? They shortened it.
jokes.setup: George Michael warns Michael about overpromising
jokes.punchline: The idiom 'give an inch, take a foot' triggers the 'threatening our feet' running gag — George Sr. interprets 'a foot' as the Chinese investors' latest threat, worrying they've escalated from feet to a specific foot
jokes.explanation: Outstanding callback execution. The 'give an inch, take a foot' idiom is perfectly positioned to trigger the foot-threat running gag. George Sr.'s 'Is it a foot now? They shortened it' implies the threat has been negotiated down, treating a common English idiom as evidence of investor communication. The interplay between the idiom's literal and figurative meanings is masterfully constructed. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: 'Some people, you give them an inch, and they take... A foot.' — George Michael's callback
jokes.setup: George Michael is warning Michael about overpromising to investors
jokes.punchline: George Michael starts the idiom 'give them an inch, they take a mile' but completes it as 'a foot' — accidentally (or knowingly) referencing the running threat gag
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's top two or three jokes. The structure is near-perfect: George Michael begins a familiar idiom and subverts its endpoint from 'mile' to 'foot' — which simultaneously (1) lands as wordplay on the feet-threat running gag, (2) works as a genuine warning, and (3) can be read as unintentional on George Michael's part, which is funnier. The economy is exceptional — the entire callback is delivered in one word swap. Michael's response ('They're threatening our feet?') then reactivates the original joke with fresh comedy. This is Arrested Development joke-craft at its finest. Justifies 8.7+ craft — this is textbook callback construction.

jokes.text: I don't want to talk about it, okay? / What? / Why don't you go back downstairs and get to work on that software floor? / Shut the door on the way out.
jokes.setup: George Michael is about to confess the truth about Fakeblock
jokes.punchline: Michael preemptively shuts down the conversation and dismisses his son, thereby preventing himself from learning that Fakeblock is fake — a moment of dramatic irony where Michael's impatience sabotages himself
jokes.explanation: Excellent dramatic irony. Michael literally cuts off the conversation that would save him — his 'I don't want to talk about it' is a preemptive defense that makes his situation worse. 'Software floor' as a term is also funny. Rewatch bonus significant.

jokes.text: Just no rush on the software, but just... ASAP. Chop, chop. / Well, not 'chop, chop,' not... That's a bad choice of words. For Christ's sake.
jokes.setup: Michael dismisses George Michael but adds urgency about the software
jokes.punchline: Michael uses 'Chop, chop' — then immediately catches himself because in the context of Chinese investors threatening to chop off feet, it's an unfortunate idiom — and panics
jokes.explanation: A very strong running gag callback. Michael's reflexive use of 'Chop, chop' followed by instant regret is perfectly timed. 'That's a bad choice of words. For Christ's sake.' is excellent — he's alone, correcting himself, and the self-recrimination is funnier than any external response could be. The foot-threat running gag is at peak form here. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: I think you're doing the right thing. We want to make the Chinese happy. Believe me, you don't want them sending your severed foot to threaten some other guy who doesn't want to build a bridge.
jokes.setup: Michael prepares to announce the wall project
jokes.punchline: George Sr. elaborates the Chinese threat into a specific nightmare scenario: they'll sever your foot and send it to someone else as a threat — an escalation of the foot-threat gag to full-on mob-movie absurdity
jokes.explanation: The foot-threat gag reaches peak elaboration: your severed foot as a warning to a third party is a perfect escalation. The specificity ('some other guy who doesn't want to build a bridge') is excellent. George Sr. delivers mob-movie logic with suburban dad energy.

jokes.text: That's what I'd do if it were one of my kids. / Did you want me to fire him for you? / No. That's nice of you. / I mean, it's not... It's not nice. Sorry.
jokes.setup: George Sr. suggests Michael should fire George Michael
jokes.punchline: George Sr. offers to fire Michael's son for him, as if this is a favor; Michael calls it 'nice' then George Sr. corrects himself — 'it's not nice' — with an odd apology for almost seeming kind
jokes.explanation: George Sr.'s correction — apologizing for seeming nice — is a sharp character beat. He can't allow himself to be perceived as altruistic and actively walks back any suggestion of kindness. Good character integration.

jokes.text: It's hard to find one where the wall builder isn't, you know, starved to death by his own wall, or dies of loneliness by his own wall. Or, of course, is crushed to death by his own wall. / Yeah. We don't want to blow the ending anyway. / Ah. Then you'll hate the one about the honeybee.
jokes.setup: A speechwriter offers to include an opening parable about a wall builder
jokes.punchline: Every wall-builder parable ends with the builder being killed by their own wall; Michael doesn't want to 'blow the ending'; the honeybee parable is then mentioned as an alternative — but the implication is it has an equally bad ending
jokes.explanation: The running catalog of wall-builder deaths is funny in its specificity (starved, lonely, crushed). 'We don't want to blow the ending' is a nice absurdist non-sequitur — treating a cautionary tale like a mystery novel spoiler. The honeybee callback at the end of the episode gives this rewatch value.

jokes.text: I need you to comply and walk over to the camera! / Comply!
jokes.setup: Gob calls his 'compliance officer' to appear on TV with him
jokes.punchline: Gob screaming 'comply!' at someone he's labeled a 'compliance officer' reveals he misunderstands what a compliance officer is — treating a person as a literal robot that complies on command
jokes.explanation: Gob's misunderstanding of 'compliance officer' as a person who literally complies with his commands is a very Gob joke — taking a business title at face value. The escalation to screaming 'Comply!' into the phone is excellent.

jokes.text: In fairness, it is a Thursday at twenty to four. / Exactly, and everyone knows the workday doesn't start until nine to five.
jokes.setup: Gob is upset that Michael scheduled an event on a weekday afternoon
jokes.punchline: Gob's complaint that Michael scheduled too early reveals he believes the workday starts at 9... to 5, as in the song — meaning he thinks work begins at the END of the Dolly Parton song's working hours, or that the 'day' starts at 5pm
jokes.explanation: 'Everyone knows the workday doesn't start until nine to five' is a spectacular Gob line. On its face it sounds like a normal statement about business hours — until you realize he's said 'doesn't start until' 9-5, meaning he thinks the workday begins at 5pm (or that 9-to-5 describes when work ends, not when it happens). It's an entirely plausible mistake for Gob to make, and the confident delivery makes it perfect. High originality, high character integration, highly quotable. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Exactly, and everyone knows the workday doesn't start until nine to five.
jokes.setup: Gob is justifying his outrage that the event was held at 3:40 PM ('twenty to four').
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'workday doesn't start until nine to five' — he appears to mean the workday starts at 9 and ends at 5, but 3:40 would still be within that window. His logic is internally broken.
jokes.explanation: This line is brilliant because Gob's logic is wrong in multiple ways: 3:40 PM is well within a 9-5 day, and 'doesn't start until nine to five' implies the workday starts at 5 PM at the earliest. His total ignorance of professional norms while posturing as a businessman is excellent character comedy. Highly quotable, very Gob.

jokes.text: You wanna see groundbreaking gay TV? Don't start without me.
jokes.setup: Michael is doing a groundbreaking ceremony; Gob hears 'groundbreaking' and interprets it differently
jokes.punchline: Gob either (1) hears 'groundbreaking' and interprets it as being about gay content on television, or (2) is referring to the literal digging as 'gay TV' — either reading is absurd. His enthusiasm for 'groundbreaking gay TV' is perfectly Gob.
jokes.explanation: Gob misreading 'groundbreaking' as both adjective (innovative) and noun (ceremony) while adding 'gay' is a classic Gob double-entendre that intersects with his Season 5 storyline. 'Don't start without me' implies he's rushing to be part of something he doesn't understand but desperately wants to participate in. High character integration. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: In a modern world where there are certainly more threats greater than a 34-year-old hard-working migrant hoping to send money home to feed his wife and three young... future rapists.
jokes.setup: Michael gives a speech about the wall project, beginning what sounds like a sympathetic framing of migrants
jokes.punchline: 'Future rapists' — Michael pivots mid-sentence from sympathetic language about migrant families to an anti-immigrant slur, apparently including rhetoric he was told to use by investors, exposing the cynicism of the whole enterprise
jokes.explanation: The structure is perfect: a full sentence of sympathetic humanizing language ('hard-working,' 'wife and three young') followed by 'future rapists' at the very end, clearly text someone else wrote for Michael. The joke's satirical target (xenophobic political rhetoric) is clear, and the punchline's placement at the sentence's end — after maximum buildup — is structurally excellent. The abruptness of the beat that follows is what makes it land.

jokes.text: Of course, I'm talking about the threat of cyber.
jokes.setup: 'Future rapists' lands as a shocking non sequitur; Michael pivots
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately pivots to 'I'm talking about the threat of cyber' — which (1) doesn't explain the previous statement, (2) reveals the speech was prepared for a different purpose, and (3) 'the threat of cyber' is a wonderfully awkward coinage
jokes.explanation: 'The threat of cyber' as an actual phrase (without specifying 'cyber what') is a pointed mockery of politicians who use 'cyber' as a vague bogeyman. The pivot also fails to address 'future rapists,' making the cover-up worse than the gaffe.

jokes.text: We meant to do this, folks, so nobody was deceived.
jokes.setup: Michael announces Fakeblock has been acquired and integrated into the wall project — surprising even himself
jokes.punchline: 'We meant to do this, folks, so nobody was deceived' — a direct address to the crowd that confirms something unplanned happened while claiming it was planned, which is itself a deception
jokes.explanation: The spin of 'we meant to do this' while clearly surprised is a good Michael Bluth moment — his instinct is always to manage optics rather than acknowledge chaos. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: My name is Gob Bluth, and as president of Fakeblock, I have an announcement! I'm building a wall! / Come back for the unveiling in two full weeks!
jokes.setup: Michael has just done a careful groundbreaking with specific messaging
jokes.punchline: Gob arrives and immediately undermines everything by counter-announcing he personally is 'building a wall' and promising a two-week unveiling — creating obligations and commitments Michael can't meet
jokes.explanation: Gob's counter-announcement is peak Gob chaos. The two-week unveiling is a perfect specific detail — it's too soon, too specific, and completely undermines Michael's careful stall strategy. The bravado of 'I'm building a wall!' after showing up late is excellent character comedy.

jokes.text: Wow, that was spectacular. / Couldn't have gone better.
jokes.setup: The groundbreaking event has gone completely off the rails
jokes.punchline: George Sr. and Lucille congratulate each other on how well it went — completely sincerely — in the face of obvious chaos
jokes.explanation: Functional irony beat. The Bluths' willful positivity in the face of disaster is a reliable joke pattern for the show, executed competently here but not memorably.

jokes.text: Come in close. I think I'm winning your mother back. I'm gonna stick it to her tonight.
jokes.setup: George Sr. pulls Michael aside after the groundbreaking
jokes.punchline: 'Stick it to her tonight' in the context of 'winning back' a romantic partner lands as a sexual double entendre — but the phrasing 'stick it to her' also means to harm/defeat someone, making his romantic intention sound aggressive
jokes.explanation: Standard double-entendre. Not as sharp as some of the show's better wordplay moments, but the 'Come in close' setup and delivery give it some comedy of intimacy.

jokes.text: I had a shovel for you. / No, it's okay. I wanted to come in hot, make a show of it. / God, did you see the look on their faces? / Just when you said two weeks to the unveiling. / Right? / And when you turned and went after them.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to process what Gob just did at the groundbreaking
jokes.punchline: Gob is delighted with his own performance, interpreting everything — including the appalled audience reaction — as triumph; 'I wanted to come in hot' as a description of arriving late to sabotage an event is perfectly Gob
jokes.explanation: Gob's post-event self-congratulation is a solid character moment. 'Come in hot' for arriving late and causing chaos is a good Gob reframing. The detail about 'the look on their faces' (presumably horror) as something Gob sees as positive is good.

jokes.text: George Michael fires Michael — 'You're fired.' / 'You have more shares than me, so you could fire me.' / 'I just did.'
jokes.setup: Michael tells George Michael he needs to make the software in a month; George Michael says he can't do it
jokes.punchline: George Michael fires his own father — Michael doesn't believe he'd do it, George Michael confirms he already did
jokes.explanation: The reversal of the father-fires-son dynamic to son-fires-father lands because the show has carefully built George Michael's growing assertiveness. 'I just did' as a three-word confirmation of something Michael didn't believe possible is a strong button. The structure is clean.

jokes.text: I was hoping to stall for six, but your uncle announced an unveiling in two weeks.
jokes.setup: Michael tells George Michael they need the software in a month
jokes.punchline: Michael's plan was to stall for six months; Gob's action has collapsed the window to two weeks — a perfect illustration of the Bluth family's collective self-sabotage
jokes.explanation: Good payoff of Gob's stunt. 'I was hoping to stall for six' reveals Michael's actual strategy was a lie, compounding the dishonesty of the situation. Solid but functional rather than exceptional.

jokes.text: I'm the only one who needs to lose feet over this... sleep over this.
jokes.setup: George Michael expresses anxiety about being the one on the line for Fakeblock
jokes.punchline: George Michael accidentally says 'lose feet' instead of 'lose sleep' — immediately catching himself, but the slip reveals the foot-threat anxiety has permeated the family subconscious
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best running gag payoff and arguably its second-best joke overall. George Michael's Freudian slip — 'lose feet' instead of 'lose sleep' — is a masterpiece of callback construction. It (1) perfectly rewards the foot-threat running gag, (2) shows the anxiety has infected even George Michael unconsciously, (3) catches itself mid-word which is the ideal timing, and (4) is completely earned by every previous iteration. Economy is near-perfect. Justifies 8+ comfortably.

jokes.text: I can tell you what your grandmother would do. Um... / You're fired. / She does go right there, but obviously I don't think that you would go that far. / You have more shares than me, so you could fire me. / I just did.
jokes.setup: Michael tries to frame George Michael's potential response to being in an impossible position
jokes.punchline: Michael preemptively dismisses 'you're fired' as too extreme for George Michael — but George Michael immediately confirms he did just fire his father, having done so calmly and already in the past tense
jokes.explanation: Perfect structure. Michael says 'obviously you wouldn't go that far' — then George Michael quietly confirms he already has. 'I just did' in past tense (having fired him during the scene) is the ideal delivery. The callback to the grandmother firing joke completes the character circle. Justifies 8+ for exceptional structural payoff.

jokes.text: Yup. This is how it ended for the honeybee, as well.
jokes.setup: George Michael fires Michael; the scene ends on Michael's defeat
jokes.punchline: The narrator calls back the speechwriter's honeybee parable — which apparently also ended with the builder being destroyed — completing the wall/builder death motif while commenting on Michael's arc
jokes.explanation: The honeybee callback is delivered with perfect economy — one sentence resolves the parable thread from 20 minutes earlier, applies it to Michael's situation, and works as a dark punchline to the episode's central storyline. The narrator's 'as well' implies the honeybee was destroyed in exactly the same way Michael is being destroyed. Very high economy score. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Buster becomes one of 2015's biggest memes. [with triumphant music, one-armed Buster dancing]
jokes.setup: The 'On the next Arrested Development' segment teases Buster's fate
jokes.punchline: Rather than legal resolution, Buster's achievement is becoming a meme — the absurdist peak of internet culture applied to a murder suspect
jokes.explanation: The 'biggest meme of 2015' as a legal outcome is a good absurdist joke. The triumphant music accompanying Buster's murder-suspect meme status is a good visual/audio gag. Dated in retrospect but works within the show's self-aware register.

jokes.text: Guys, guys, guys, this fits with the fact that he can't walk. / And has been carried everywhere he went, damn it. / Which means what, G-Man? / He couldn't have committed this crime. In any way.
jokes.setup: The Guilty Guys make a breakthrough about shoe evidence
jokes.punchline: Their major legal discovery is that Buster can't walk — a fact presumably known to everyone since Season 1 — presented as a shocking forensic breakthrough
jokes.explanation: The procedural drama of discovering Buster can't walk as a forensic breakthrough is a good joke about both the Guilty Guys' incompetence and legal dramatics. 'In any way' as a clarifying addition is a nice touch.

jokes.text: Get that box of evidence over to The Bluth Company with the bill. / They're billing hours. Get our time sheets in.
jokes.setup: The Guilty Guys make a breakthrough exonerating Buster
jokes.punchline: Even mid-breakthrough, the immediate priority is billing hours and getting time sheets in — the legal billing machine overrides any satisfaction from the discovery
jokes.explanation: Good institutional joke about lawyer billing culture. The parallel tracks of 'justice' and 'billing' running simultaneously is sharp. The immediate pivot to time sheets undermines the dramatic breakthrough.

jokes.text: As they clear a man... / They're billing hours. Get our time sheets in. / ...who had died two years earlier.
jokes.setup: The Guilty Guys celebrate exonerating Buster with forensic evidence
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals in passing that the person they've been exonerating died two years ago — the entire legal defense has been on behalf of a dead man, and no one noticed
jokes.explanation: The episode's best structural joke and one of the show's finest cold-close moments. The narrator drops 'who had died two years earlier' completely casually — as a parenthetical — while the Guilty Guys continue billing. The structure is impeccable: the Guilty Guys' breakthrough is interrupted mid-sentence by the reveal that their client is dead. The intercut with 'They're billing hours' before the revelation makes the joke even darker — they're billing while defending a corpse. The casualness of delivery ('as they clear a man...who had died two years earlier') is perfect. This is a legitimate 9+ joke — it retroactively recontextualizes everything and delivers maximum impact with minimal words. Justifies 8+ by a wide margin.

tmdb_overview: Buster's brief appearance in a TV weather report puts the Bluths back in hot water, but Michael has a plan involving his son's definitely real company.
insight: Season finale relies heavily on character quirks but lacks the callback density that defined earlier runs.
